Staffing
The Library manages day-to-day operations with an ever-shrinking number of staff members. The
Library started out the last four years with a staff of just three people:
• Marcia Eblen, Library Technician (12 month, Classified)
• Linda Kiely, Library Technician (12 month, Classified)
• Jude Baldwin, Librarian (195 days, Faculty)
Marcia Eblen retired in June 2020 and Linda Kiely retired at the end of the Fall 2020 semester. The
Library now functions with a staff of just two people:
• James Wilcox, Library Technician II (12 month, Classified)
• Jude Baldwin, Librarian (195 days, Faculty)
Library staff reports to the Dean of Liberal Arts in Academic Affairs. Deans during this time period
were:
• Burt Peretti (September 2019 – March 2020)
• Valerie Roberts (March 2020 – current)
Who We Serve & How We Serve Them
As our mission statement mentions, the Library primarily serves College of the Siskiyous’ students,
faculty, and staff. We secondarily serve our community members who are not currently enrolled in
classes and visiting researchers. We serve our campus and wider community through our collections,
services, and physical and online spaces.
The Library serves our campus by providing collections relevant to campus needs. Our main goals in
managing our collection are to:
1. Support students with their class work
2. Support faculty preparation for classes
3. Support college employees in their professional duties
4. Support students and employees in life-long learning and reading
5. Preserve local history
Collection materials can be physical or electronic. Physical collections include books, reserve textbooks,
audio recordings in a variety of formats, DVDs, maps, periodicals, and ephemera. Electronic collections
include online articles, ebooks, and streaming videos and other media (such as e-audiobooks); these
are usually packaged together and accessed as databases. The Library also now manages the campus
laptop and graphing calculator checkout programs.
Equally as important are the many library services staff are able to provide. These include:
• Research and information literacy instruction using a wide variety of methods
• Direct research assistance
• Computer technical support
• Interlibrary loan
More details about our instruction program and direct research assistance are covered in other areas
of this program review.

The Library can be contacted in a wide variety of ways, including new methods added during the last
four years:
• In person during library hours
• Phone
• Email
• Drop-in Zoom hours (added Spring 2020)
• Text (added Summer 2020)
• Chat via our web site, the library catalog, and EBSCO databases (added Summer 2022)
As a facility, the Library offers quiet study space, a group study room, and a casual “lounge” area that
doubles as our event space. Food and drink are allowed everywhere in the Library. Our space also
provides a home for our physical collections, as well as staff areas and storage. Our space contains
necessary equipment such as computers, printers (black & white and color), copier, scanner, microfilm
reader, and media players.
The Library manages an online “space” as well through our pages on the COS web site, where we
provide general FAQs, self-serve how-to information regarding research in the form of handouts and
videos, and information to support faculty. Our web site also contains our library catalog search
interface and access to our article databases and other online collections.
Library Operations as Percent of the College’s Budget
The Library provides these all of these resources and services to all students and employees all year
round with a small budget that is only 1% of the College’s expenditures. Other than staff, our
collections (primarily in Capital Outlay), which include databases and the software required to manage
them, are our largest expenditures.

2. External Factors with Significant Impact
The COVID-19 Pandemic
The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on library operations during this time period cannot be ignored.
The College of the Siskiyous campus shut down in March 2020 and most instruction was moved online;
in person classes did not really start to return in significant numbers until Fall 2021 (face-to-face
enrollment continues to be down from pre-pandemic levels). Most employees worked remotely. The
stress of this time was high for many people—the risk of getting sick, the death toll of the disease, job
and financial insecurities, lack of child care and at-home K-12 remote learning, contentious politics,
and general uncertainty—made 2020-2021 especially difficult times.
In terms of library operations, the pandemic had both positive and negative impacts. Let’s get the
negative out of the way first:
• Prompted the retirement of library staff
• Lack of direct connection with students
• Could not serve students who were regular users of our space
The Library’s physical space was open on and off between March 2020 – March 2022 due to pandemic
fluctuations and/or staff shortages.
• Spring 2020 (starting in March) & Summer 2020: physical library closed
• Fall 2020: physical library open with reduced hours
• Winter 2021, Spring 2021, & Summer 2021: physically closed (only one staff person left)
• Fall 2021: physical library open with reduced hours (only one staff person left but Library re-
opened in expectation that a second staff member would be hired; this did not happen until
February)
• Winter 2022: physical library closed
• Spring 2022: physical library open with reduced hours initially, increased to full business hours
mid-semester (after new hire was trained)
On the positive side, the pandemic gave us the opportunity to try new things, many of which have
been very successful:
• Implemented texting, Zoom, and chat services as contact methods
• Expanded library-generated instruction
• Started a circulating reserves collection
• Started to purchase required reading materials as ebooks and e-audiobooks when available
• Increased database offerings
• Strengthened partnerships with other areas on campus, especially the Academic Success Center
(ASC) and Basecamp
Staffing Challenges
As it has been mentioned several times, library staffing has become an increasing challenge. In 2008,
the Library had a staff of seven: five classified staff, one faculty librarian, and one full-time, year-round
librarian who served as director. Staff numbers have been shrinking since then. By 2012, the Library no
longer had a full-time, year-round library director but an Assistant Dean who served oversaw the
Library, Distance Learning, and other areas (Nancy Shepard); no full-time faculty librarian (the position

had been vacant since 2008); and three classified staff (one position was eliminated in 2009, the other
position was not re-filled after the incumbent retired in 2010). 2012 saw two other staff changes when
a part-time faculty librarian was hired (Jude Baldwin) and a classified staff position was eliminated
(Martha Berryman’s position).
The Library eliminated the part-time faculty librarian position when it was able to add a full-time
faculty librarian position in 2014 (Jude Baldwin). In November 2016, the Assistant Dean of Learning
Resources (Nancy Shepard) retired and administrative oversight was transferred to the Dean of Liberal
Arts & Sciences position in the Instruction office.
Marcia Eblen, who had worked at COS for over 30 years, retired in June 2020 and Linda Kiely (hired in
2016) retired at the end of the Fall 2020 semester (technically, her last day was in January 2021 but she
ceased working in December). Marcia Eblen’s position was eliminated in the staff restructuring under
college President Dr. Schoonmaker. The Library successfully re-wrote and upgraded the classification of
the Library Technician position from a Level I to Level II; James Wilcox started in this new position in
February 2022. Jude Baldwin was the sole library employee for all of 2021 and January of 2022.
Operating with just three staff members was limiting and operating with just two is even more so.
Technological changes in library operations and a scaling back of hours and services over the years has
allowed us to function with dwindling staff. While the Library may be functioning adequately, we are
not thriving. Some of the impacts of our current staffing levels are that it:
• Limits open hours
• Causes occasional closures for lunch or longer (unstable hours)
• Limits increased engagement with students through events
• Limits staff participation in shared governance (when only one person is working it can be
difficult/impossible to fully participate in meetings and trainings)
• Limits librarian’s availability to develop new micro-courses (the Library’s very successful self-
paced instruction program)
• Limits librarian’s availability to develop new EdTALKS
• Significantly slowed our full implementation of OneSearch (the LSP/library catalog)
• Limits expansion of self-serve instructional web site content
• Limits creation of cross-references in our collection to increase use of materials
• Limits our ability to participate in campus table events
• Increases our reliance on series and pre-made collections when purchasing books
• Decreases our ability to weed outdated materials from our collections so that they better serve
student needs
• Limits our ability to actively recover checked-out materials from students in a timely manner
The Library has repeatedly asked for funding to increase staff in various ways. While the College has
been responsive about granting funds to cover faculty librarian adjunct contracts for Summer and
Winter Sessions, most requests for employee funding have been unsuccessful, as these program
review Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (CQIP) examples show:

o Requested funds to increase the length of the faculty librarian contract (not funded)

Library vs. the Internet
Current traditional college-aged students have never known a time without the internet and its wealth
of free information, often presented in an easily digested format. Trying to communicate and convince
students of the value of library resources like databases is difficult. A finding from the Library Student
Focus Group report put it bluntly: “Students in this group much preferred the internet and saw the
library as a dated resource.” This may explain the waning “strongly agree/agree” responses to the
annual student question, “Having used the COS Library’s resources, services, and instruction, I
appreciate the value of libraries in supporting student success,” which has decreased from a high of
98% in 2019 to 87% in 2023 (although 87% is up from the pandemic low of 77%).
Budget Shortfall Predictions
The Library budget has been relatively flat for the last four years and we have only needed to make
minor cuts to cover our rising operating costs. We have been fortunate to receive supplemental
funding from non-District sources such as CARES/HEERF funds during the pandemic, Guided Pathways
funds, Instructional Equipment funds, and Foundation Mini-Grants. College budget predictions vary
and include some grim scenarios. The Library keeps financial sustainability in mind when making many
financial decisions.

1. SLO 1: Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will be
able to effectively locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
2. SLO 2: Students who participate in formal research instruction will be able to evaluate for
credibility information provided by the library, Internet resources, and other information
sources.

3. SLO 3: Students who use the COS Library and utilize its resources, services, and formal
instruction will appreciate the value of libraries in supporting academic and/or career success
and in supporting life-long learning.
When assessing, the Library uses the Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate
to determine whether each SLO is being met:
• Transfer courses (such as ENGL 1001): ≥67%
• Credit courses (such as ENGL 0900): ≥67%
• Basic Skills courses (such as ENGL 0850): ≥45%
• Vocational Courses: ≥70%
Common methods for gathering data include:
• students answering “strongly agree” or “agree” on post-class session surveys or the Annual
Student Survey
• Library Micro-Course quiz completion scores
• students scores on activities or assignments
SLO 1: Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will
be able to effectively locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
ENGL 1001 discussion posts
Since Fall 2021, Liz Carlyle’s online ENGL1001 classes have a discussion assignment asking students to
choose keywords for a topic of their choosing and to find two articles using the library’s EBSCO
databases. Liz has imported and modified the Library’s research modules from the Canvas Commons
which cover how to get to and select a database, how to use the basic features of EBSCO, and how to
choose basic keywords. Discussion posts were analyzed to see if students were able to meet the
assignment criteria, showing an ability to effectively locate needed information using library sources.
Of the students who attempted the assignment, most students were able to find two database articles
(92%) and identify reasonable keywords for their topic (95%). Many students (21) did not supply
keywords as per the assignment instructions. A student was considered not able to identify reasonable
keywords if they vastly oversimplified their topic (for example, a topic on the impact the COVID-19
pandemic on the music industry was searched as simply music) or if they used sentence fragments
more suitable to natural language searching. Overall, these ENGL 1001 students demonstrate being
able to meet this SLO. Please see Appendix A for full assessment data.
ENGL 1001 Post-Class Visit Student Surveys
During this time period the Library conducted evaluations after visits with Liz Carlyle’s ENGL 1001
classes. Students reported that the Library visits helped them to feel more confident in getting to the
databases (90%), finding articles in EBSCO (99%), choosing keywords (78%), and using advanced search
techniques (85%). Their responses showed that they were meeting this SLO. Please see Appendix B for
full survey data.

Library Micro-Courses
The Library launched the Library Micro-Courses (LMCs) in Spring 2022. Five of the LMCs offered since
then support SLO #1:
1. COS Library Basics
2. Getting Started with Library Research
3. EBSCO Article Database Basics
4. EBSCO Hands-On Practice
5. Keyword Searching Basics
The quiz at the end of each micro-course has a total of 10 possible points. Because quizzes are
untimed, repeatable, and open book, the Library considers a score of 8 to be “passing.” Student scores
on the end-of-course quizzes for these micro-courses average between 8.51 to 9.34, demonstrating
that they are meeting these SLO.
The Library opens a new LMC Canvas shell twice a year at the start of the Fall and Spring semesters.
Before opening them to students, the quizzes are reviewed using the Quiz Statistics feature in Canvas.
Questions that students frequently get wrong are modified or replaced in a way that hopefully
resonates more with students.
Student feedback about the micro-courses has been positive and many students have expressed that
they learning something new from the micro-course. The number of student participants has grown
each semester since they launched, from just 23 students in Spring/Summer 2022 to 98 in Fall/Winter
2022-23 to 122 students in Spring/Summer 2023. Much of this growth is due to the COLL 0600
requirement that students complete a certain number of EdTALKS each semester. The micro-courses
are considered an EDTALK for this purpose and students seem to appreciate the convenience of an
online 24/7 option to meet this requirement instead of a synchronous EdTALK workshop.
Please see Appendix C for LCM SLOs and assessment data.
Annual Student Survey
The Library collects feedback about this SLO through a question on our Annual Student Survey:
Survey Question: The library provides information and instruction for students to help them
develop good research skills.
Previously the survey was collected in the Library, Student Center, and Lodges on the Weed Campus,
the Main Office and/or ASC on the Yreka Campus, as well as online but since 2020 the survey has been
conducted entirely online. Results from this question show that students think the Library could be
doing more to help them develop their research skills, although the numbers do meet the criteria of
the assessment plan standards. Student satisfaction in this area dropped during the pandemic which is
understandable since instruction offerings decreased, especially class visits. After a low in 2021 of 66%,
student satisfaction is on the rise, a trajectory that will hopefully continue. During that time there has
also been a rise in students claiming to be neutral and who answer “Don't Know or N/A”; the number
of students expressing dissatisfaction (Disagree/Strongly disagree) has returned to pre-pandemic levels
after a bump in 2020-2021.

4. Library Quantitative & Qualitative Data
Research & Information Literacy Instruction
In the last four years, the Library has focused on growing our research and information literacy
instruction offerings. Traditionally, library instruction happened primarily through classroom visits to
support student assignments. While this point-of-need instruction is very valuable, how frequently it
happens is determined by faculty. Requests for class visits dropped by more than half during the
pandemic and have not recovered: only 61 classroom visits were conducted in the past four years
compared to 148 in the previous four-year period. It should be noted that the overall number of
classes being offered is also down and the number of in-person classes—where most class visits
occur—has not recovered to pre-pandemic levels.

In Spring 2023, 113 students took 347 micro-courses. That semester there were eleven micro-courses
to choose from, with COS Library Basics (55 completions) and Getting Started with Library Research (54
completions) being the most popular, followed by Scholarly Conversation, Plagiarism & Why We Cite
(48 completions) and EBSCO Article Database Basics (45 completions). Least popular were the micro-
courses covering APA citations. Students are given the opportunity to provide anonymous survey
feedback after each micro-course, which thus far has been positive. In the survey, students are allowed
to give a star rating to each course, which is published on the micro-courses home page for future
students to see. So far, all courses have received a rating of 4.5 out of 5 stars or above. Considering
both the number of students participating, the satisfaction students express, and their contribution to
meeting our learning outcomes, the Library Micro-Courses are a success!

class resource during hands-on class research time.
General Use of the Library’s Collections
Before the pandemic, students taking our Annual Student Survey respond that our collections, both
physical and electronic, were doing well at meeting their needs, with 92% of respondents in 2019
answering Strongly Agree or Agree. Student opinions sank during the pandemic to 66% (2021) but they
are on the rise again, up to 77% in 2023.

The Library currently spends about half of its budget (excluding personnel costs) on materials for our
collections. About 75 percent of collection costs are for online subscriptions and 25 percent for
physical materials. Of the physical materials we purchase, an increasing percentage is going towards
textbooks as we continue to grow the circulating textbook reserves collection. Online collections are
cost effective and are readily available to all students 24/7, regardless of location. This balance appears
to be generally effective and helps us to meet Accreditation Standard IIB1.
Circulating Reserve Textbooks
The Library started this time period with a traditional textbook reserve collection: a set of most of the
textbooks being used in COS classes that were available for use in the COS Library. We also had a small
collection of textbooks for use on the Yreka Campus, selected in consultation with the Yreka-based
counselor and using class schedules for Yreka-based classes (including videoconferenced classes).
The pandemic made the college aware of how many students struggled to get their textbooks and
brought with it an influx of funds that made purchasing textbooks possible. Textbooks from both
campuses were consolidated into one working collection and annual District funds spent on textbooks
increased by about $1,000, but it was CARES (Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security)/HEERF
(Higher Education Emergency Relief Fund) funds that made building a circulating textbook collection
possible.

Now that the Library has a core collection of two copies of most textbooks (one for in-library use, one
for checkout), we purchase by request since student demand changes every semester. We estimate
that it will take about $10,000 in FY23-24 to be able to honor most student textbook requests. This
purchasing includes ebooks as well as physical textbooks. Library ebooks can be quite expensive,
especially since we strive to have at least three concurrent user access to each title in order to serve an
entire class. For example, a “regular” book such as There There, by Tommy Orange (used in ENGL 1001)

costs $85 for one user and a “traditional” textbook such as Nutrition for Healthy Eating, by Wendy
Schiff (used in FCS/KINE 1011) costs $711 for three concurrent users.
In the OER Workgroup’s survey (Spring 2022), students shared that they prefer print textbooks (64.4%
of respondents) over ebooks (20.2% of respondents), however the use statistics for our ebook
textbooks shows that they are using them at very high rates (see Database Use section below).
Considering that students also admit that in order to reduce textbook costs they have resorted to not
purchasing materials at all (36.2%) and taking fewer classes (27.7%), perhaps the fact that the ebooks
are free overrides their format preference. When asked “If the College has the financial resources to
help reduce your textbook costs (if a free textbook option is not available), what methods would help
you the most?”, library ebooks (94 responses) were not too far below physical library books (104
responses).
Ebook Database Use
The Library’s online databases are the most used part of our collection, with thousands of uses each
semester. Database statistics use a standard called COUNTER in order to make comparisons between

Reference questions have been gradually declining in the Library and, like many of our statistics, took a
large hit during the pandemic. One theory about the pre-pandemic decline was that some may be due

Checkouts
The Library disaggregated the data about students who checked out library materials. The Library
looked at all checkouts in general and reserve, laptop, and calculator checkouts in particular. We used
data from January 2020 (our first semester “live” with our new software system) through Spring 2023.
It should be noted that the Library did not start to manage the campus laptop and calculator lending
programs until Spring 2023.
Race/Ethnicity disaggregation: In general, the percent of students checking our materials mirrors
overall COS demographics with regards to race/ethnicity. The exceptions are African Americans who
check out materials in general, reserve books, and laptops at a higher percentage rate compared to
their percent of the overall COS population. The same is true of students reporting “two or more
races.” White Non-Hispanic and Asian students check materials of all kinds out at lower rates
compared to their percent of the overall COS population.
Gender disaggregation: In general, the percent of students checking out materials mirrors overall COS
demographics with regards to gender.

-- 20 of 58 --

title: 2023-2024 Library Comprehensive Program Review (Part 21)
text:
21
Age disaggregation: In general, students in the 20-24 age group are checking out materials at much
higher percentages compared to their percent of the overall COS population. Students up to 19 and 20-
24 make up 70-80% of circulation across all categories, while combined they are only 53% of the
overall student body. It is unknown whether this is because older students are not aware of what the
library has to offer or if they already have, or can afford to buy the materials they need for their
classes, or some other factor.
Database Logins
EZproxy Analytics (EZPA) data combined with data from ZogoTech Analytics shows that the
race/ethnicity of the students logging into our online collections are similar to the overall student
population. The same was not true of gender and age group characteristics. Female students logged in
at a higher rate than male students as compared to their percent of the overall COS population.
Students in the 20-24 age group also logged in at much higher rates.
Most of the database research assignments the Library is aware of are in English (in particular, ENGL
1001), Early Childhood Education, and Psychology classes. ENGL 1001 demographics with regards to
race/ethnicity and gender mirror the overall COS student population, although this class is heavily
skewed towards students aged 19 or less. Interestingly, the students identified as majoring in English,
Early Childhood Education, and Psychology are primarily female (81% of students) and are much more
likely to be in the 20-24 age group than the overall student body (28% of majors vs. 19% of student
body). This may explain the differences in the login data, although it should be noted that these majors
are also predominantly White Non-Hispanic, Two or More Races, or Not Reported and, other than
possibly Two or More Races, this does not appear to have impacted the race/ethnicity percentages of
the students logging in.
Library Satisfaction
In Spring 2022 and Spring 2023, the Library added a race/ethnicity question to our Annual Student
Survey, which was conducted in partnership with the ASC (2022 and 2023), the OER Workgroup (2022),
and Basecamp (2023). Looking specifically at students who self-identified as African American/Black,
Latino/a/x / Chino/a/x, or Native American/Alaskan Native, these groups in general reported
satisfaction rates that were higher than the rates from all responses. Some exceptions include:
• African American students were less satisfied in Spring 2023 regarding library access (survey
question 4 (Q4) and library instruction in finding credible sources (Q6).
• Latino/a/x students were much less satisfied with the Library’s collections in both surveys. They
are the only group that had lower satisfaction ratings (compared to the overall rates) regarding
the Library as a safe, comfortable environment (2022) and regarding the value of the Library to
student success (2022 & 2023).
• Native American/Alaskan Native students were less satisfied in 2022 in almost all categories
but in 2023 their satisfaction improved to higher than the overall survey rates in all categories.
The survey gives students the opportunity to provide narrative feedback regarding library services.
Most students left this optional item blank or made positive comments such as “I feel with my
experience with the library you guys do a fine job assisting students” (Latino/a/x student) and “love it”

Non-library survey data
In Fall 2023, the Educational Master Plan team conducted a student and employee survey to collect
opinions on various aspects of the college. One question on the survey asked “What do you believe are
the greatest strengths of the college?” and allowed respondents to choose all that applied. Out of the
28 available options, “library services” ranked near the top with both groups.
• 28 out of 46 employee respondents (61%) chose “library services” as one of the College’s
greatest strengths; the only options ranked higher were “helpfulness of staff” (33), parking and
Basecamp (both 30), and CTE programs (29); library services were tied with tutoring services.
• 63 out of 121 student respondents (52%) chose “library services” as one of the College’s
greatest strengths; the only options ranked higher by students were “helpfulness of staff” (85),
“student services” (80), “welcoming and well-maintained appearance of campus” (70), and the
web site (67); library services were tied with Basecamp services.
The same survey also asked employees and students what areas of the college needed improvement.
• Among employees, the Library received the lowest number of responses out of all 28 options
(only one person selected “library services”).
• Among students, the Library received the second lowest number of responses out of all 28
options (only three people selected “library services”); the only other option to receive fewer
student responses was “fellow students and colleagues.”
It is gratifying to see that both our colleagues and students consider the Library and what we offer as a
strength and without much need for improvement.
5. Progress on Prior Goals
The Library’s Four-Year Action Plan had four broad categories, each with identified action steps. While
the pandemic forced us to abandon or modify some action steps, progress was made on all goals.
a) The Library will have adequate staffing to meet our workload needs and provide
adequate open hours
While the Library completed most of our action steps, this did not result in adequate staffing levels.
The restructuring that took place in early 2020 eliminated a library position; the Library was not

-- 23 of 58 --

title: 2023-2024 Library Comprehensive Program Review (Part 24)
text:
24
informed of this until long after the fact when we tried to rehire positions. This combined with the
challenges of the pandemic were obstacles to meeting the intent of this goal.
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities for students, including
offering a credit-bearing Research Skills and/or Information Literacy course
The Library made great strides in growing our instruction program during this time period. While staff
shortages and the pandemic prevented us from launching a credit-bearing course as planned, it will be
offered for the first time in Spring 2024. The Library Micro-Courses have been very successful and have
significantly increased our instructional reach, which has been especially helpful in light of declining
class visits.
c) The Library will have greater student engagement from developing a student-
friendly space, from adapting to the ways students use technology, from increased
activities, and through access to resources that meets student needs
Understaffing and the COVID-19 pandemic impeded progress on this goal: most action steps are
considered to still be in progress and will be carried forward into our next Four-Year Action Plan. The
pandemic did bring with it CARES/HEERF funds which were helpful: most completed action steps
involved purchasing, such as furniture, laptops, and movie night equipment.
d) Increase faculty engagement with the library
While many action steps were completed, the Library still hears from students that they don’t hear
about library services from faculty. Also, faculty do not often use the library space or services
themselves, take advantage of library flex activities, or reach out to us for collaboration. The most
successful engagement the Library has had with faculty are collaborations on events held in the Library
and the Library plans to continue looking for opportunities to do this. The Library will also include
reviving the Library Advisory Committee in our next Four-Year Action Plan, since this avenue has not
yet been explored.
e) Improve our collections
The Library made several steps towards improving our collections. Students are excited about the
circulating textbook collection developed during the pandemic and the e-textbooks the Library has
been able to provide. The Library did not make any progress towards creating a collection
development plan for the Mount Shasta Collection or the college “archives”—the pandemic made
other actions a higher priority—and so these activities will continue onto our next Four-Year Action
Plan.
Details about actions steps taken are included in Appendix G.
6. Closing the Budget Loop
Other than requests for funds related to hiring or additional compensation for employees, the Library
has been successful in the last four years with regard to extra funding requests. These funding requests
are primarily submitted during the Program Review process or budget development as CQIPs.
Examples of successful CQIP requests from the last four years include:

-- 24 of 58 --

title: 2023-2024 Library Comprehensive Program Review (Part 25)
text:
25
• The Library has received adjunct funding so that a librarian can be reasonably available during
the summer and/or winter sessions
• The Library received funds to improve our technology, such as laptops for student use in the
Library and equipment for movie nights and other events
• The Library has received funds for furniture upgrades
• The Library has been able to add science databases, filling a gap in our database collection
• The Library was granted a small increase in our budget to hire student employees
Unsuccessful requests for staffing were addressed section 2. External Factors with Significant Impact:
Staffing Challenges.
In our AY22-23 Annual Update, the Library requested funds for:
1. A 244-day librarian contract (CQIP unsuccessful)
2. A part-time Library Technician I (CQIP unsuccessful)
3. An increase in funds to hire student employees (CQIP successful)
4. To conduct a student focus group (CQIP withdrawn: funding found elsewhere)
The Library did not receive funds for the longer librarian contract or to restore a part-time position,
which made the additional student employee funds very helpful. Our FY22-23 funding for student
employees ($3,181) only covered student employee wages through the Fall semester, so an increase to
$8,270 in FY23-24 was very welcome. Student employees provide much needed support to library staff
including physical processing of materials, designing displays and advertising, shelving of materials, and
assistance with circulation and directional questions.
The Library was able to conduct two focus groups in Spring 2023 using funds from Guided Pathways to
purchase food during the sessions and participation incentives. The focus groups were conducted by JT
Tarantino and Makenna Rivers in Planning, Assessment & Research. While the focus group information
was interesting, unfortunately it was often not specific enough for the Library to have clear direction
on ways to improve. Key general takeaways from the focus groups were that:
• Students are interested in opportunities for fun ways to interact with the Library, both in terms
of our collections and activities/events
• The Library needs to do more marketing since many students did not realize that we already
offer services they wanted
• Students are not learning about library services from their instructors, which is another
marketing opportunity
• Students would like more open hours
• Students do not always recognize the value of the Library’s collections (vs. the internet)
The Library has addressed these focus group key takeaways in our Four-Year Action Plan.
In addition to the funding requests generated by program review process, the Library also submitted a
funding request in the budget development process to increase the amount our budget for circulating
textbook reserves. This request was approved using Lottery funds. More information about the impact
of circulating textbook reserves is above in section 4: Library Quantitative and Qualitative Data:

title: 2023-2024 Library Comprehensive Program Review (Part 26)
text:
26
More details about funding requests are included in Appendix H.
7. Four-Year Vision
The Library will move from surviving to thriving by providing expanded and improved supports for
student engagement and success.
8. Four-Year Action Plan
Over the next four years, from AY2023-24 to AY2026-27, the Library will focus on the following areas to
help us to achieve our vision:
a) The Library will have adequate staffing
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities
c) The Library will have greater student engagement
d) The Library will increase marketing of our space, collections, and services to students
e) The Library will have greater faculty engagement
f) The Library will improve our collections
a) The Library will have adequate staffing
The Library needs more staff in order to meet our basic workloads needs and to increase instructional
offerings, the number of engagement activities offered (movie night events, etc.), and open hours.
Action Steps
• Increase number of staff working in the Library by adding a part-time classified position by the
end of AY26-27
• Increase the Faculty Librarian contract from 195 to 244 days by the end of AY26-27
• Learn more about the “50% Law” (California Education Code §84362) and how the Library fits in
with this: can we strengthen our arguments for these positions and benefit the college?
How success will be measured
• The Library has fewer closures due to staff shortages (as compared to years where we
functioned with just two staff members; statistics started to be kept in AY23-24)
• The Library is able to exceed the number of new micro-courses and EdTALKS created/offered
(see “increased instructional activities” goal for details)
• Increase of any amount of additional open hours, including additional “special occasion” hours
(midterms, Thanksgiving break, etc.), evening hours, earlier morning opening hours, etc.
• Student satisfaction on our annual survey will increase from Spring 2023 levels, especially on
questions 3, 4, and 6
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities
The Library will continue to grow our instructional offerings, which allows us to support the college’s
mission of offering “inclusive and practical learning experiences that are relevant in today’s global
economy and environment” and inspiring students to “engage actively, question deeply, reason
critically, think boldly, communicate effectively, act innovatively, work collaboratively, and live
ethically.”

-- 26 of 58 --

title: 2023-2024 Library Comprehensive Program Review (Part 27)
text:
27
Action Plan
• Offer LIBR 1001 (1 credit) at least once by the end of AY24-25
• Determine viability of a 3-credit information literacy course which would meet the criteria for
our new local GE pattern (Area 5) and CSU-GE (Area E), which cover Lifelong Learning & Self-
Development, by the end of AY24-25; submit curriculum as needed by the end of AY26-27
• Review and revise the Library’s three student learning outcomes by the end of AY26-27
• Expand the Library Micro-Courses by adding one new micro-course or significantly revising an
existing micro-course every semester
• Expand library EdTALKS by adding one new EdTALK each academic year
• Investigate collaboration with COLL 0600, such as whether the Library can be an allowable
study space and research appointments an allowable activity (similar to meeting with an
academic coach/tutor) by the end of AY24-25
• Offer student research appointment booking by the start of AY24-25
How success will be measured
• The Library will have more instructional offerings for students by meet our goals for LIBR 1001,
Library Micro-Courses, and EdTALKS
• The Library will have more micro-courses that align to our updated SLOs than at the end of
AY22-23 (5 out of 11 micro-courses)
• The Library will have at least one opportunity to interact with COLL 0600 students (other than
EdTALKS or LMC’s)
• Student satisfaction on our annual survey will increase from Spring 2023 levels on questions 3
and 6
c) The Library will have greater student engagement
The Library would like to increase student engagement, a phrase which has many definitions. In this
context, the Library is interested in interacting with students outside of direct support for classroom
assignments, but within the scope of the Library’s mission, so that students build positive associations
with the Library. This positive relationship is intended to make students feel like the COS Library is a
place where they belong and where they feel comfortable visiting and/or asking for academic support
when needed. Hopefully this college experience will also make students feel like libraries in general are
a place where they belong and a place that can support their life-long learning and recreational needs.
The Library has broken this lofty goal down into actions such as activities and events, actively seeking
student input, and by creating a student-friendly space.
Action Steps
• Offer one event each semester, such as movie nights, poetry/open mic nights, concerts, talks,
and exhibits (i.e. something bigger than the standard library book display)
• Increase student club use of the Library for meetings and events to at least one per semester
(in addition to Club Spectrum meetings, which already use the study room)
• Identify ways to engage with online students such as through a newsletter or online displays
• Continue to upgrade our physical space, ideally with student feedback, by making one new
change to our space every academic year, such as new furniture, new décor, significant

reconfiguration of existing furniture, creation of solo study areas or a standing desk,
improvement of entrance, etc.
• Research ideas for making our space friendlier to students with disabilities by the end of AY23-
24; implement at least three ideas by the end of AY26-27
• Increase recreational use of our collections (see “improve our collections” section for details)
• Replace student desktop computers, which are from 2014, by the end of AY24-25
• Include students on the Library Advisory Committee (see “increase faculty engagement”
section)
How success will be measured
• The Library’s gate count will have increased back to AY19-20 levels by the end of AY26-27
• Student satisfaction on our annual survey will increase from Spring 2023 levels on questions 2,
5 and 7; data will be disaggregated by Student Access Services (SAS) enrollment if possible
d) The Library will increase marketing of our space, collections, and services to students
Action Steps
• Send out a minimum of one social media post per month during the Fall and Spring semesters
advertising our space, collections, and services (not events)
• Create marketing materials targeting specific audiences such as SAS students, Hispanic
students, Yreka-based students by the end of AY25-26; include specific measurement(s) of
success in planning
• Advertise Library mini-visits at the beginning of the semester to provide a 5-minute overview of
library collections and services
• Improve Library signage within the Library (reference, etc.) and in the LRC lobby, perhaps in
partnership with other LRC areas (ASC, Basecamp, Career Center) by the end of AY26-27;
consider signage for the stacks to aid with browsing
• Provide textbook marketing materials for the Bookstore every semester starting with Fall 2024
• Design at least one marketing effort each academic year for Lodge students regarding Library
space, collections, and services
How success will be measured
• The Library’s gate count will have increased back to AY19-20 levels by the end of AY26-27
• Materials circulation will increase from AY22-23 levels; this overlaps Goal F regarding improved
collections
• Student satisfaction on our annual survey will increase from Spring 2023 levels on question 7;
data will be disaggregated by target marketing audience if possible
e) The Library will have greater faculty engagement
Faculty are a key Library user group. Our collections and services are designed to support their
students and curriculum and they are an important connection point between the Library and
students.
Action Steps
• Develop an annual faculty survey and implement by the end of AY24-25; include benchmark for
determining faculty satisfaction with library services

• Update the Library information in the New Faculty Canvas course by June 30, 2024
• Revive the Library Advisory Committee for the start of AY24-25
• The Library will consult with Theatre faculty by the end of Spring 2024 and develop a plan with

• The Library will consult with Music faculty by the end of Spring 2025 and develop a plan with

f) The Library will improve our collections
Action Steps
• Research pulling fiction books into their own section by the end of AY24-25; create
implementation plan by the end of AY26-27 (if needed)
• Start game checkout by the end of AY23-24; purchase at least 5 new games by the end of AY24-
25; determine circulation success metrics based on the number of games we have available
• Research offering a “library of things” for student checkout, including best practices for this
type of collection and what types of items students want, by the end of AY24-25; create
implementation plan by the end of AY25-26 (if needed)
• Research offering a “zip books” program, similar to the county library system by the end of
AY24-25; create implementation plan by the end of AY25-26 (if needed)
• The number of laptops available for semester loan will increase to 100 by the end of AY26-27
• Increase the number of technology peripherals available for in-library use and for checkout,
such as laptop charging cables, charging/connection adapters, speakers, noise cancelling
headphones, standard/trackball/touchpad mice, rolling backpack, special keyboards,
podcasting microphone; purchase and start circulating at least three new technology
peripherals by the start of Fall 2024
• By the end of AY25-26, determine if the Library should keep or expand interlibrary loan (ILL)
services by analyzing lending/borrowing statistics, estimated staff time on day-to-day activities
(current and estimated if expanded), estimated staff time in integrating ILL into Alma if
expanded, and estimated shipping costs of expansion
• The Library’s database collection at the end of AY26-27 will be at a similar level of quantity,
quality, and coverage to AY23-24, with appropriate adjustments made to adapt to curriculum
needs
• Create a collection development plan for the college “archives” that takes into account staffing
and budget considerations by the end of AY26-27
• Create a collection plan for the Mount Shasta Collection that takes into account staffing and
budget considerations by the end of AY26-27
• Create and implement an accessibility plan for handling database article and ebook accessibility
issues and to make the courtesy textbook scans we provide accessible by the start of Fall 2024

How success will be measured
• The Library will not have a laptop wait list of more than 10 students
• If the Library creates a fiction area, circulation statistics for these materials will increase from
the levels in the two academic years prior to the move
• If the Library starts a circulating collection of “things”, games, and/or technology peripherals,
the Library will determine what level of usage is successful and track circulation statistics
• If continued, ILL borrowing statistics will increase
• Overall database use statistics will, at a minimum, hold steady at AY22-23 usage levels
• Student satisfaction on our annual survey will increase from Spring 2023 levels on questions 1
and 2
9. Conclusion
The COS Library is doing well in light of the challenges of the past four years. We use a variety of
methods to fulfill our mission to support student learning and college employees. In doing so, we also
support the mission of the college and are meeting accreditation requirements. While we are doing
well, we are often in “survival mode”: due to low staffing we cannot do much beyond the basics. In
order to thrive, the Library will need more staff and continued funding support. We have identified
several areas where we can improve, expand, and adapt in order to to meet our campus community’s
needs in the four years ahead.

Additional action steps taken to support Goal c) Description
Eliminate overdue/late fines Many academic libraries are eliminating overdue
fines as a way to remove barriers to library use
and be more equitable. The COS Library is in the
progress of going fine-free: AP 4040 was
updated; once AP 5030 has passed the Board we
will be officially be “fine free,” eliminating
overdue/late fines on all circulating materials,
including reserves, and the processing fee on
unreturned/damaged materials.
Semester-long checkouts In researching the elimination of fines, the
Library found several studies that indicated
longer checkout periods could also eliminate a
barrier that prevents some students from using
the Library. The Library has implemented
semester-long checkout periods for “regular”
books since the pandemic started and intends to
continue. Circulating textbooks also check out for
the semester.

Table 42: Additional actions taken to meet the Four-Year Action goal regarding collection improvement
Additional action steps taken to support Goal e) Description
Free mailing services To improve access to our physical collections, the
Library now offers free mailing services to
students who do not have access to the Weed or
Yreka Campuses. Students are also supplied with
a free return shipping label so that all mailing
costs are free for them. Most materials mailed
are textbooks
Purchasing e-textbooks The Library has begun to purchase e-textbooks
whenever possible. These are expensive,
especially for materials from traditional textbook
publishers such as McGraw-Hill. Many textbooks
are not yet available to libraries as ebooks. E-
audiobooks are also being purchased when they
are available to provide students with options
that may better suit their learning preferences.
The ebook use statistics are fantastic; e-
audiobook has thus far been light.
These ebooks are very expensive. It is unclear
whether the College will continue to support a
collection of circulating reserve books now that
CARES/HEERF funds are gone (they were used to
make the initial purchases). The e-audiobooks
are also getting low use and the subscription
model used by this vendor (OverDrive) does not
suit our needs very well. Continued access to this
collection also needs to be reconsidered.
Faculty as campus OER Liaison The faculty librarian volunteered to be the new
campus OER Liaison. This has prompted
increased coordination with the Bookstore, more
involvement with faculty, and additional
outreach activities for students (such as the
Textbook Connection EdTALK). This has also
provided the Library an opportunity to raise
awareness about ZTC library ebook textbook
options.
